meta.notes: Source is the Saylor Foundation 'General Chemistry I MCQ' ebook (course CHEM101), distributed as a QuizOver.com PDF under CC BY-NC-ND 3.0. The PDF presents each question and its four answer choices but WITHHOLDS the answer key (it links to QuizOver.com to 'check the answer'), so every item here is answerProvenance=verified-from-source-text — the correct answer was derived independently by calculation or established concept (mirroring how questions.json handles OpenStax items whose keys are withheld). Choices are reproduced verbatim in the order printed and labeled A-D. LICENSE NOTE: CC BY-NC-ND is open but non-commercial + no-derivatives; downstream commercial shipping should confirm rights. Fills the thermochemistry/thermodynamics gap not covered by the GOB LibreTexts book.

questions.stem: A 5.10 g sample of iron is heated from 36.0 °C to 75.0 °C. The amount of energy required is 89.5 J. What is the specific heat capacity of iron?

hints.prompt: What equation relates heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat?

choices.label: A
choices.text: q = m x c x (delta T)

hints.rationale: Heat q = mass x specific heat x temperature change.

hints.prompt: Solving q = mc(delta T) for c with q = 89.5 J, m = 5.10 g, and delta T = 75.0 - 36.0, which setup is right?
hints.rationale: c = q / (m x delta T), with delta T = 39.0 degrees C.

hints.prompt: Specific heats of metals are small (well under 1 J/g degrees C). Which two choices can be eliminated as far too large?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 17800 J/g degrees C and 11.7 J/g degrees C

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0.900 J/g degrees C and 0.450 J/g degrees C

choices.label: C
choices.text: 17800 J/g degrees C and 0.450 J/g degrees C

choices.label: D
choices.text: 11.7 J/g degrees C and 0.900 J/g degrees C

hints.rationale: Dividing about 90 J by (5.10 g x about 39 degrees C) gives a value below 1, so 11.7 and 17800 are impossible.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key (links to QuizOver.com); answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: A 6.75 g sample of gold (specific heat capacity = 0.13 J/g °C) is heated using 50.6 J of energy. If the original temperature of the gold is 25 °C, what is its final temperature?
questions.explanation: From q = m·c·ΔT, ΔT = q / (m·c) = 50.6 J / (6.75 g × 0.13 J/g·°C) = 57.7 °C. Final temperature = 25 °C + 57.7 °C = 82.7 °C.

hints.prompt: Which equation lets you find a temperature change from the heat added?

choices.label: A
choices.text: q = m x c x (delta T), so delta T = q / (m x c)

choices.label: B
choices.text: delta T = q x m x c

choices.label: C
choices.text: delta T = m x c / q

hints.rationale: delta T = q / (m x c).

hints.prompt: After finding delta T, how do you get the final temperature when heat is ADDED to gold starting at 25 degrees C?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Final T = 25 degrees C + delta T

choices.label: B
choices.text: Final T = 25 degrees C - delta T

choices.label: D
choices.text: Final T = 25 degrees C x delta T

hints.rationale: Adding heat raises the temperature, so final = initial + delta T.

hints.prompt: Which choice is the trap equal to the temperature CHANGE (delta T) rather than the final temperature?
hints.rationale: 57.7 degrees C is delta T; the final temperature must add this to the 25 degrees C start.

questions.stem: A negative Gibbs free energy means a reaction will
questions.explanation: A negative Gibbs free energy (ΔG < 0) indicates a thermodynamically spontaneous (product-favored) process. ΔG says nothing about reaction rate (that is kinetics), so 'react quickly' is not implied.

hints.prompt: Which property of a reaction does the Gibbs free energy change (delta G) describe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the reaction is thermodynamically favorable - not how fast it occurs

choices.label: C
choices.text: The color change during the reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of moles of gas produced

hints.rationale: Delta G addresses thermodynamic favorability, which is separate from reaction rate.

hints.prompt: Does delta G say anything about how FAST a reaction proceeds?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - rate is a kinetic property, separate from delta G

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes - a negative delta G means the reaction is fast

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes - delta G is the rate constant

hints.rationale: delta G governs spontaneity, not speed.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because delta G says nothing about reaction speed?

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Both A and B' and 'None of the above'

hints.rationale: Speed is kinetic, so 'react quickly' - and 'both', which includes it - are wrong.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 06: Thermochemistry and Thermodynamics
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified (definitional).

questions.stem: Exothermic reactions are favored because they:
questions.explanation: An exothermic reaction releases heat to the surroundings, which increases the entropy of the surroundings (ΔS_surr = -ΔH_sys/T > 0 when ΔH_sys < 0). By the second law, this contributes to making the overall process spontaneous.

hints.prompt: According to the second law of thermodynamics, what must happen for a process to be spontaneous?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The total entropy of the system plus its surroundings must increase

hints.rationale: Spontaneity depends on the total entropy change of the system and its surroundings.

hints.prompt: For an exothermic reaction delta H(system) is negative, and delta S(surroundings) = -delta H(system)/T. What is the sign of delta S(surroundings)?
hints.rationale: With a negative delta H(system), -delta H(system)/T is positive.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they refer to the system's entropy rather than the surroundings'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Increase entropy of the system' and 'Decrease entropy of the system'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Increase entropy of the surroundings' and 'Decrease entropy of the surroundings'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Increase entropy of the surroundings' and 'Increase entropy of the system'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Decrease entropy of the surroundings' and 'Decrease entropy of the system'

hints.rationale: The favorability of exothermic reactions comes from heat raising the surroundings' entropy, so the system-entropy options do not apply.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 06: Thermochemistry and Thermodynamics

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: For the following general reaction, what can be said about the spontaneity at different temperatures? A + B → C + D; ΔH° is positive; ΔS° is negative.
questions.explanation: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. With ΔH° > 0 and ΔS° < 0, the term -TΔS is positive at every temperature, so ΔG > 0 for all T. The reaction is nonspontaneous (product not-favored) at any temperature.

hints.prompt: What is the Gibbs free energy equation used to judge spontaneity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: delta G = delta H - T(delta S)

choices.label: B
choices.text: delta G = delta H + T(delta S)

choices.label: C
choices.text: delta G = T(delta H) - delta S

choices.label: D
choices.text: delta G = delta H x delta S

hints.rationale: delta G = delta H - T(delta S) determines spontaneity.

hints.prompt: With delta H > 0 and delta S < 0, what is the sign of the term -T(delta S), given that T is always positive?
hints.rationale: Negative times a negative delta S, times positive T, is positive for all T.

hints.prompt: Because delta G stays positive at every temperature, which two choices can be eliminated as impossible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'product-favored only at high temperature' and 'product-favored only at low temperature'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'product-favored at all temperatures' and 'product not-favored at any temperature'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'product-favored at all temperatures' and 'product-favored only at high temperature'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'product-favored only at low temperature' and 'product not-favored at any temperature'

hints.rationale: There is no temperature crossover when both terms push delta G positive, so the 'only at high/low temperature' options are impossible.

questions.stem: Which answer provides the calculation of ΔG° for the reaction below at 25 °C? H2O(g) + S(s) → H2S(g) + 1/2 O2(g); ΔH° = +221.2 kJ, ΔS° = +87.7 J/K
questions.explanation: ΔG° = ΔH° - TΔS° = 221.2 kJ - (298 K)(0.0877 kJ/K) = 221.2 - 26.1 = 195.1 kJ. Convert ΔS° from J/K to kJ/K and use T in kelvin (25 °C = 298 K).

hints.prompt: Which equation and unit care are needed to compute delta G from delta H and delta S?

choices.label: A
choices.text: delta G = delta H - T(delta S), converting delta S to kJ and using T in kelvin

choices.label: B
choices.text: delta G = delta H + T(delta S), with T in degrees C

choices.label: D
choices.text: delta G = T(delta S) - delta H

hints.rationale: Use delta G = delta H - T(delta S) with consistent units (kJ and kelvin).

hints.prompt: Plugging in delta H = 221.2 kJ, T = 298 K, and delta S = 0.0877 kJ/K, which setup is correct?
hints.rationale: Convert delta S to kJ/K and use T = 298 K: 221.2 - 298 x 0.0877.

hints.prompt: The T(delta S) term (about 26 kJ) is subtracted from a positive delta H. Which two choices can be eliminated (from adding it, or from making delta G negative)?
hints.rationale: 247.3 kJ comes from adding T(delta S), and a negative result reverses the correct sign of delta G.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: Use the following thermochemical equations to solve for ΔH° for the reaction 2 F2(g) + 2 H2O(l) → 4 HF(g) + O2(g). [Given: H2(g) + F2(g) → 2 HF(g), ΔH° = -542 kJ; 2 H2(g) + O2(g) → 2 H2O(l), ΔH° = -572 kJ]
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, target = 2 × (first equation) - (second equation). 2 × (-542) = -1084 kJ; reversing the second equation contributes +572 kJ. ΔH° = -1084 + 572 = -512 kJ.

hints.prompt: What does Hess's law let you do with thermochemical equations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add, scale, or reverse known reactions - scaling or reversing changes delta H accordingly

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ignore the delta H signs when reversing an equation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only add equations exactly as written

choices.label: D
choices.text: Simply average the delta H values

hints.rationale: You may manipulate equations; reversing flips the sign of delta H and scaling multiplies it.

hints.prompt: The target needs 2 times the first equation plus the reverse of the second. How do their delta H values combine?
hints.rationale: Double the first delta H and reverse the sign of the second: 2(-542) + 572.

hints.prompt: Reversing the second equation contributes +572 kJ. Which two choices can be eliminated because they fail to add this positive term (leaving them too negative)?
hints.rationale: Starting from 2 x (-542) = -1084 kJ and adding +572 kJ moves the result well above -900 kJ, so -922 and -1124 are too negative.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified by Hess's law.

questions.stem: What is the sign of the enthalpy change for an exothermic reaction?

choices.label: B
choices.text: This cannot be determined from the information given.

questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release heat to the surroundings, so the enthalpy change of the system (ΔH) is negative.

hints.prompt: What does the SIGN of a reaction's enthalpy change (delta H) tell you?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the reaction releases heat (exothermic) or absorbs heat (endothermic)

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of moles of gas

hints.rationale: The sign of delta H distinguishes heat-releasing reactions from heat-absorbing ones.

hints.prompt: Which distractor wrongly suggests the sign of delta H cannot be known for an exothermic reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'This cannot be determined from the information given'

hints.rationale: 'Exothermic' by definition fixes the sign, so 'cannot be determined' is a distractor.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because 'exothermic' already fixes the sign of delta H?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'This cannot be determined from the information given' and 'It depends on the temperature'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'positive' and 'This cannot be determined from the information given'

hints.rationale: The definition of exothermic sets delta H's sign, so the 'undetermined' and 'depends on temperature' options are wrong.

questions.stem: What is the value of the standard enthalpy of formation for any element under standard conditions?
questions.explanation: By definition, the standard enthalpy of formation (ΔHf°) of an element in its most stable (standard) form is zero, because forming an element from itself involves no change.

hints.prompt: What does the standard enthalpy of formation of a substance describe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The enthalpy change when it forms from its constituent elements in their standard states

hints.rationale: Standard enthalpy of formation is the enthalpy change of forming a substance from its elements in standard states.

hints.prompt: For an element that is ALREADY in its standard state, how much of a formation reaction is needed to 'form' it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: None - there is no change to carry out

choices.label: C
choices.text: A change equal to its molar mass

hints.rationale: An element in its standard state requires no formation reaction, so there is nothing to change.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated as unrelated physical constants rather than an element's formation enthalpy?
hints.rationale: 273 and 4.18 echo the Celsius-Kelvin offset and water's specific heat - neither defines an element's formation enthalpy.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified (definitional).

questions.stem: Which of the following do you expect to have the largest entropy at 25 °C?
questions.explanation: Gases have much higher molar entropy than liquids or solids because of their greater molecular disorder. O2 is the only gas among the choices, so it has the largest entropy.

hints.prompt: How does entropy generally compare among solids, liquids, and gases?
hints.rationale: Gases have the highest molar entropy because of their greater disorder.

hints.prompt: When comparing the entropy of different substances, which factor usually dominates - the physical state or the specific compound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The physical state; gases have far higher entropy than liquids or solids

choices.label: B
choices.text: The specific compound always dominates over the state

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neither has any effect on entropy

hints.rationale: Physical state, especially being a gas, dominates molar-entropy comparisons.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are condensed (solid or liquid) phases with lower entropy?
hints.rationale: Solids and liquids have much lower molar entropy than gases.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 06: Thermochemistry and Thermodynamics
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following represents an increase in entropy?

choices.label: C
choices.text: crystallization of salt from a supersaturated solution

questions.explanation: Boiling converts liquid water to vapor, greatly increasing molecular disorder and therefore entropy. Freezing and crystallization produce more ordered solids (entropy decreases), and combining 2 mol of gas into 1 mol of gas also decreases entropy.

hints.prompt: Which kind of change increases a substance's entropy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moving to a more disordered state, such as liquid to gas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Moving to a more ordered state

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reducing the number of gas molecules

hints.rationale: Entropy rises when disorder increases, as in vaporization.

hints.prompt: For a phase change, moving in which direction increases entropy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Toward the gas phase (solid to liquid to gas)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Toward the solid phase (gas to liquid to solid)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phase changes do not affect entropy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only a temperature change can affect entropy

hints.rationale: Entropy increases as a substance moves toward the more disordered gas phase.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they produce a more ordered (lower-entropy) state?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Freezing of water and crystallization of salt from a supersaturated solution

choices.label: B
choices.text: Boiling of water and the reaction 2 NO(g) -> N2O2(g)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Freezing of water and boiling of water

choices.label: D
choices.text: Crystallization of salt from a supersaturated solution and the reaction 2 NO(g) -> N2O2(g)

hints.rationale: Freezing and crystallization form ordered solids, decreasing entropy.
